title: What is a goroutine
instruction: A goroutine is a lightweight task Go runs alongside your main code. Start one with the `go` keyword. Run this — the search queries and patron names print interleaved because they run concurrently.
successMessage: Searches and patrons mixed together — two functions running simultaneously. The `go` keyword is all it took. Goroutines are incredibly cheap.

title: Channels — communicate between goroutines
instruction: A channel is a pipe between goroutines. One goroutine sends a result, another receives it. Run this — a goroutine looks up a book and sends the result back through the channel.
successMessage: The goroutine looked up the ISBN and sent the result back through the channel. No shared variables, no locks — just a channel.

title: Send and receive multiple values
instruction: A channel can send as many results as you need. Start three goroutines, each finding a book by ISBN. Add a third goroutine for ISBN 978014 and receive its result.
hint: go findBook(978014, ch)  then result3 := <-ch  fmt.Println(result3)

codeChecks.message: Receive the third result from the channel.
Print the third book result containing ISBN 978014.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Three goroutines, three results, one channel. The results may come back in any order — the channel collects them as they finish.

title: WaitGroup — wait for goroutines to finish
instruction: `sync.WaitGroup` lets you wait for a group of goroutines to complete. Run this. Three volunteers each process a shelf section. `wg.Wait()` blocks until all three call `wg.Done()`.
successMessage: wg.Wait() blocked until all three goroutines called wg.Done(). WaitGroup is the standard way to coordinate a group of goroutines.

title: Buffered channels
instruction: A regular channel blocks until the receiver is ready. A buffered channel has a queue — `make(chan string, 3)` lets you send up to 3 values before anyone receives them. Run this.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	ch := make(chan string, 3)

	ch <- "The Catcher in the Rye"
	ch <- "1984"
	ch <- "To Kill a Mockingbird"

	fmt.Println(<-ch)
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
}
successMessage: No goroutines needed — the buffer held all three books. Buffered channels decouple senders from receivers.

title: Range over a channel
instruction: You can loop over a channel with `range` — it receives values until the channel is closed. `close(ch)` signals that no more results are coming. Run this.
successMessage: range on a channel is clean and safe. The loop exits the moment the channel closes. Always close from the sender side.

title: Mutex — protect shared data
instruction: When multiple goroutines access the same variable, use `sync.Mutex` to prevent race conditions. Run this — a library counter is checked out by 100 patrons safely.
successMessage: 100 patrons, 100 check-outs, exactly 100 every time. Without the mutex you would get random results between 1 and 100.

title: Race condition — spot and fix it
instruction: The code below has a race condition on `count`. Run it a few times — the result varies. Fix it by adding a `sync.Mutex` to protect `count`.
hint: Add var mu sync.Mutex above the loop. Inside the goroutine: mu.Lock(); count++; mu.Unlock() — or use defer.

codeChecks.message: Add a sync.Mutex to protect the count variable.

successMessage: Check-outs: 50, every single time. The mutex serialises access to count. Run `go test -race` to catch race conditions automatically.

title: Build a concurrent pipeline
instruction: Build a three-stage concurrent book processing pipeline: `findBooks(ids ...int) <-chan string` sends formatted titles, `categorize(in <-chan string) <-chan string` prepends a genre, `head(in <-chan string, n int) <-chan string` limits output. Display the first 5 categorized books.
hint: Each stage: make a channel, start a goroutine that reads from in and writes to out, returns the out channel. The head stage should use a counter and close early.

codeChecks.message: Define a findBooks function that returns a channel.
Define a categorize function that categorizes items from a channel.
Define a head function that limits items from a channel.
Each stage should return a <-chan string (receive-only channel).
Close the output channel when your goroutine is done.
Use range to read from channels in each stage.

successMessage: A three-stage concurrent pipeline. Book IDs flow through stages — found, categorized, limited — each running concurrently. This is how Go handles data streaming at scale.
